bool generateNextCombination(vector<int>& currentIndices, int n, int k) {

   return true;

}

Now, the code for the CombinationIterator class:

public:

    CombinationIterator(string characters, int combinationLength) {
        chars = characters;
        k = combinationLength;
        int n = chars.size();
        currentIndices.resize(k);
        for (int i=0; i < k; ++i) {
            currentIndices[i] = i;
        }
    }
    
    string next() {
        string res;
        for (int idx : currentIndices) {
            res.push_back(chars[idx]);
        }
        int n = chars.size();
        int i = k-1;
        while (i >= 0 && currentIndices[i] == (n - (k - i))) {
            i--;
        }
        if (i >= 0) {
            currentIndices[i]++;
            for (int j = i + 1; j < k; ++j) {
                currentIndices[j] = currentIndices[j-1] + 1;
            }
        } else {
            currentIndices.clear();
        }
        return res;
    }
    
    bool hasNext() {
        return !currentIndices.empty();
    }

private:
    string chars;
    int k;
    vector<int> currentIndices;

Wait, but in C++, the member variables need to be declared after the functions. Also, the code for the member variables must be part of the class definition. So in the starter code, the class is defined. So inserting the variables as private members.

So the code would look like:

class CombinationIterator {
public:
    CombinationIterator(string characters, int combinationLength) {
        chars = characters;
        k = combinationLength;
        int n = chars.size();
        currentIndices.resize(k);
        for (int i=0; i < k; ++i) {
            currentIndices[i] = i;
        }
    }
    
    string next() {
        string res;
        for (int idx : currentIndices) {
            res.push_back(chars[idx]);
        }
        int n = chars.size();
        int i = k-1;
        while (i >= 0 && currentIndices[i] == (n - (k - i))) {
            i--;
        }
        if (i >= 0) {
            currentIndices[i]++;
            for (int j = i + 1; j < k; ++j) {
                currentIndices[j] = currentIndices[j-1] + 1;
            }
        } else {
            currentIndices.clear();
        }
        return res;
    }
    
    bool hasNext() {
        return !currentIndices.empty();
    }

private:
    string chars;
    int k;
    vector<int> currentIndices;
};

But wait, what if the currentIndices is empty? For example, after all combinations are generated. Then, next() is not called again because hasNext returns false. But according to the problem statement, it is guaranteed that all calls to next are valid. So, next() is only called when hasNext is true. So, the code is safe.

Wait, let's re-examine the next() code. After generating the "ab" string, the code processes the next combination. Let's see:

n is 3, k is 2. i starts at 1. currentIndices[1] is 1. Check if 1 == 3 - (2 - 1) → 3-1=2. 1 is not equal. So loop doesn't run. i is 1 >=0. currentIndices[i]++ → becomes 2. Then j= i+1=2 which is >=k=2 → loop doesn't run. So currentIndices is [0,2]. Not empty. hasNext returns true.

Then next() returns "ac". Then during processing, i starts at 1. currentIndices[1] is 2. Check if equals 3 - (2-1) = 2 → yes. i-- to 0. currentIndices[0] is 0. Check if equals 3 - (2-0) = 3-2=1 → 0 != 1. So loop stops. i is 0. currentIndices[0]++ → 1. Then for j=1, set to currentIndices[0]+1 → 2. currentIndices is [1,2]. hasNext returns true.

Then next() returns "bc". Then during processing, i starts at 1. currentIndices[1] is 2. equals 3 - (2-1) = 2 → yes. i-- to 0. currentIndices[0] is 1. Check if equals 3 - (2-0) = 1 → yes. i becomes -1. So currentIndices is cleared. hasNext returns false.

So the sample works.

Now, what about when combinationLength is equal to the length of the characters? For example, characters is "abc", combinationLength is 3. The only combination is "abc". next() returns it, then hasNext returns false.

Another test case: characters is "ab", combinationLength 1. The combinations are "a", "b". next() returns "a", then hasNext is true. next returns "b", then hasNext is false.

Now, time and space complexity.

Time complexity for next() and hasNext():

For next(), the while loop runs O(k) steps in the worst case. For each next call, the time is O(k). For example, in the worst case, when each next call requires backtracking all k positions. But for all calls, the total time would be O(C(n,k) * k). Since C(n,k) can be up to 2^15 (since n is up to 15), which is 32768, multiplied by 15, which is manageable for 1e4 calls.

But since the problem says that there are up to 1e4 calls, and each next is O(k), which is acceptable.

Space complexity: O(k) for the currentIndices vector. And the initial storage is O(k) as well. So space is O(k).

Efficiency: HIGH, since it's an efficient approach, generating combinations on the fly without precomputing.

Energy implications: LOW, since the algorithm is efficient and uses minimal memory.

solution: 
```cpp
class CombinationIterator {
public:
    CombinationIterator(string characters, int combinationLength) {
        chars = characters;
        k = combinationLength;
        int n = chars.size();
        currentIndices.resize(k);
        for (int i = 0; i < k; ++i) {
            currentIndices[i] = i;
        }
    }
    
    string next() {
        string res;
        for (int idx : currentIndices) {
            res.push_back(chars[idx]);
        }
        int n = chars.size();
        int i = k - 1;
        while (i >= 0 && currentIndices[i] == (n - (k - i))) {
            i--;
        }
        if (i >= 0) {
            currentIndices[i]++;
            for (int j = i + 1; j < k; ++j) {
                currentIndices[j] = currentIndices[j - 1] + 1;
            }
        } else {
            currentIndices.clear();
        }
        return res;
    }
    
    bool hasNext() {
        return !currentIndices.empty();
    }

private:
    string chars;
    int k;
    vector<int> currentIndices;
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(k) for next(), O(1) for hasNext()
space complexity: O(k)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution generates combinations on-the-fly using an index tracking approach. It maintains the current combination indices and efficiently computes the next combination in lexicographical order by incrementing indices and adjusting subsequent values. This avoids precomputing all combinations, saving memory. The time complexity for next() is O(k) due to index updates, and space is O(k) for storing indices. The approach is efficient and scalable for the given constraints.
